title: Gelatin surface sizing
subtitle: Dipping handmade sheets in a warm gelatin bath to create a writing surface
excerpt: Gelatin surface sizing has been the standard preparation for European writing paper since the 14th century. A warm dilute gelatin bath deposits a thin protein film in the surface fibres, transforming absorbent water-leaf paper into a smooth, pen-receptive sheet. This tutorial covers the bath preparation, dipping sequence, and drying method.
sourceNotes: Timothy Barrett, 'European Papermaking Techniques  to ', in Paper Through Time: Nondestructive Analysis of 14th- through 19th-Century Papers (2016, University of Iowa Libraries). Gelatin bath ratios and temperature guidance follow Barrett's workshop documentation.

glossaryTerms.definition: A container of dilute size solution into which unsized paper sheets are dipped to receive a surface coating. The bath must stay at the correct temperature throughout the sizing session.

Adding a small quantity of potash alum to a gelatin bath to improve the hardness and water resistance of the dried size film. Historical gelatin size typically included alum at 10-15% of the gelatin weight.

content.text: must be kept at 40-50°C throughout the dipping session: too cool and the gelatin gels in the bath and on the sheet; too hot and the fibres over-expand and the sheet wrinkles severely on drying. Use the papermaking vat as the bath container if it holds heat reasonably; otherwise use a large roasting tin inside a larger container of hot water as a bain-marie.

Soak 4 g of leaf gelatin in 200 ml of cold water for 5 minutes. Then heat gently, stirring, until the gelatin is fully dissolved. Add this to 1.8 litres of warm water in your vat, the final bath is approximately 2% gelatin by weight. Optionally add
at 0.4 g per litre (about 0.8 g total in this bath) for a harder, more water-resistant result. Keep the bath at 45-50°C: too cool and the gelatin sets before penetrating the fibre.

Hold a dry sheet at one short edge with both hands. Lower the sheet into the bath at a 30° angle, then flatten it just below the surface and immediately lift it out on the other side. The dip takes three seconds: long enough for the bath to penetrate the surface fibres, short enough that the sheet does not absorb so much gelatin that it becomes stiff. Hold the sheet over the bath and let it drip for 30 seconds.

Pin or clip each dipped sheet at the top two corners to a line or frame, allowing the sheet to hang and dry freely. Do not dry flat: the weight of the wet gelatin stretches the sheet evenly when hanging, but causes uneven shrinkage on a flat board. Sheets are dry in 30-60 minutes at room temperature. They will feel slightly stiffer and smoother than the water-leaf originals.

Write a test line with a dip pen and iron-gall ink. A well-sized sheet produces clean, non-feathered line edges; ink should dry within 5 seconds. If letters still feather slightly, give the sheet a second quick dip and rehang. If the sheet feels very stiff and the ink sits proud on the surface rather than absorbing, the bath was too concentrated, dilute it by 25% for the next batch.
